en.extract:
The painted finch (Emblema pictum) is a common species of estrildid finch found in Australia. The painted finch acquired its name due to the red and white spotted and mottled underparts of both males and females. The binomial comes from emblema meaning 'mosaic or inlaid work'; and pictum derives from the Latin word pictus, meaning 'painted' (from pingere, 'to paint'). Other names include Emblema finch, mountain finch, painted firetail and Emblema. The painted finch is a popular bird to be kept in captivity and in backyard aviaries.


Taxonomy
The painted finch is within the genus Emblema which early studies placed four species inside of. More recent research has since determined that this genus did not form a natural assemblage and three of the four species were segregated into the genus Stagonopleura. The species include S. bella (Beautiful firetail), S. oculata (Red-eared firetail) and S. guttata (Diamond firetail). This species, Emblema pictum, is currently the only one allied to the genus.
The painted finch belongs to the family Estrildidae which consists of small passerine birds that occur naturally in the old world including Africa, southern Asia and Australasia. There are approximately 124 species of estrildid finches within 30 different genera. There has been two major radiations within this family, one occurring in Africa and one in Australia. The family Estrildidae is believed to have originated in India which then later dispersed to these two continents.


Description
The painted finch is a small passerine that is 10–12 cm (3.9–4.7 in) in length and weighs around 11.5 g (0.41 oz). Male painted finches have a red forehead and face that stand out in contrast to the black breast of individuals. A bright red patch in the middle of the breast is present, while the sides of the breast, belly and flanks are black with white spots. The tail and upper body and upper wing are a reddish-brown colour. The rump and uppertail coverts are also mostly red and highly conspicuous when the birds is in flight. Painted finches have a long, slender and pointed bill which in the male the upper mandible is mostly black with a red tip and the lower mandible is mostly red with light blue-grey patches on either side of the base. The Iris of males is a cream or off-white colour and the legs of males vary between a dark brown to pinkish colour.
Females look similar to males, although the red colouration on the face is duller and is restricted to the lores, cheeks and around the eyes. The red patch present on the breast is also duller when compared to the males as are the underparts that are generally a duller brownish-black. The underparts on females though are more spotted. The bill of females is quite similar to males but usually with less red on upper mandible. The iris is also a cream or off-white colour and legs once again vary between dark brown to a pinkish colour.
Juvenile painted finches are similar in appearance to females but their underparts are duller and browner. Juveniles lack the red colouration on their face and their bill is black which becomes paler and almost pinkish on the lower part. Juvenile's eyes are a grey-brown.


Ecology


Behaviour
Painted finches usually occur in pairs and small flocks; however, larger flocks of up to 100 individuals have been seen when congregating around water sources. These large flocks may contain other species of finches and honeyeaters. Painted finches are generally less vocal than other Australian grassfinches; however, its calls are among the loudest and harshest of this species group. Its contact calls have been described as sounding like trut, chek-chek or ced up, cheddy-up. When alone, males often make a loud wheezing and chattering song, females make a rattling call in response to danger near the nest. 


Diet
The painted finch feeds on grass seeds mostly from spinifex (Triodia) species and sometimes may consume fruit and blades of grass. Individuals forage on the ground, hopping and bouncing among rocks and tussocks of grass in order to glean for fallen seeds. Captive birds have seen to sally-strike flying termites and glean aphids from vegetation; however, feeding on insects in the wild has not been observed.


Reproduction
If conditions are suitable painted finches breed at almost any time of year with eggs being recorded in all months except November and December, and nestlings being recorded between March and October. Courtship usually occurs on the ground and involves both sexes picking up twigs or other items and dropping them. A greeting display may replace this which involves the male singing to the female in a vertical posture, raising its body feathers and pivoting its head from side to side.
Painted finches are monogamous and nest in simple pairs. Nesting sites are usually well concealed clumps of spinifex grass, however pairs have been seen to nest in natural cavities near the top of a clump or on the ground at the base. Nesting sites may occasionally also include other species of tussock grasses or in low shrubs. Individuals build loose, bottle shaped or domed nests with a wide entrance and usually consists of stems of spinifex but may also contain other grass stems, twigs or rootlets. The nest is often lined with feathers, plant down, hairy seeds, grass, wool, fur or balls of fluff. The opening of the nest is often decorated with a piece of charcoal.
Females will lay a clutch size of 3 to 5 oval white eggs. Eggs sometimes have a bluey tinge and are incubated for around 13–14 days by both the male and female. Young are altricial and do not leave the nest until they are around 21–26 days old. Young are fed for around two more weeks and they begin to develop courtship behaviour at 10 weeks old.


Habitat and distribution
The painted finch is found in arid and semi-arid zones, in rocky areas with a ground cover of spinifex grass. The painted finch can be found in Western Australia, Northern Australia, Queensland and South Australia   In 2007 and 2008 large influxes of painted finches entered into western New South Wales. These individuals have since been recorded breeding and a resident population is now thought to have been established.


Conservation status
On the IUCN Red list the painted finch is listed as being of "Least Concern", and this is also its listing in Queensland under the Nature Conservation Act of 1992.


References


External links
BirdLife DataZone species factsheet

The '''painted finch''' ('''''Emblema pictum''''') is a common species of [[estrildid finch]] found in [[Australia]]. The painted finch acquired its name due to the red and white spotted and mottled underparts of both males and females. The binomial comes from [[wikt:emblema|emblema]] meaning 'mosaic or inlaid work'; and ''pictum'' derives from the Latin word ''pictus'', meaning 'painted' (from [[wikt:pingere|pingere]], 'to paint').<ref name="John">{{cite book|last1=Gould|first1=John|title=The birds of Australia Part 7|date=1842|publisher=The Author|location=London}}</ref> Other names include Emblema finch, mountain finch, painted firetail and Emblema. The painted finch is a popular bird to be kept in captivity and in backyard aviaries.<ref name="cayley">{{cite book|last2=Cayley|first2=Neville W. (illustrator)|first1=Klaus |last1=Immelmann |author-link=Klaus Immelmann |title=Australian finches in bush and aviary|date=1985|publisher=Angus & Robertson Publishers|location=London|isbn=0-207-14165-7|edition=Fully rev.}}</ref><ref name="Immelman3">{{cite journal|last1=Immelmann|first1=K|title=Drought adaptations in Australian desert birds|journal=Proceedings of the International Ornithological Congress|date=1963|volume=13|pages=649–657}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
The painted finch is within the genus ''Emblema'' which early studies placed four species inside of.<ref name="Harrison">{{cite journal|last1=Harrison|first1=C.J.O.|title=The taxonomic position of the Crimson Finch and the Red-browed Finch.|journal=Emu|date=1963|volume=63|issue=1|pages=48–56|doi=10.1071/MU963048 |bibcode=1963EmuAO..63...48H }}</ref><ref name="immelmannb">{{cite journal|last1=Immelmann|first1=K|title=Contributions to a comparative biology of Australian finches|journal=Zoologische Jahrbücher Systematik|date=1965|volume=90|pages=1–196}}</ref> More recent research has since determined that this genus did not form a natural assemblage and three of the four species were segregated into the genus ''[[Stagonopleura]]''. The species include ''S. bella'' ([[Beautiful firetail]]), ''S. oculata'' ([[Red-eared firetail]]) and ''S. guttata'' ([[Diamond firetail]]). This species, ''Emblema pictum,'' is currently the only one allied to the genus.<ref name="christidis" /><ref name="christidis9">{{cite journal|last1=Christidis|first1=L.|title=Chromosomal evolution within the family Estrildidae (Aves) I. The Poephilae|journal=Genetica|date=November 1986|volume=71|issue=2|pages=81–97|doi=10.1007/BF00058691|s2cid=33942111}}</ref>

The painted finch belongs to the family [[Estrildidae]] which consists of small [[passerine]] birds that occur naturally in the old world including [[Africa]], southern [[Asia]] and [[Australasia]]. There are approximately 124 species of [[estrildid]] finches within 30 different genera.<ref name="goodwin">{{cite book|last1=Goodwin|first1=Derek|title=Estrildid finches of the world|date=1982|publisher=British Museum Natural History|location=London|isbn=978-0-565-00832-1}}</ref><ref name="christidis">{{cite journal|last1=Christidis|first1=L|title=Phylogeny and Systematics of Estrildine Finches and Their Relationships to Other Seed-eating Passerines|journal=Emu|date=1987|volume=87|issue=2|pages=119–123|doi=10.1071/MU9870119|bibcode=1987EmuAO..87..119C}}</ref> There has been two major radiations within this family, one occurring in [[Africa]] and one in [[Australia]]. The family [[Estrildidae]] is believed to have originated in [[India]] which then later dispersed to these two continents.<ref name="goodwin" /><ref name="christidis" />

==Description==
The painted finch is a small passerine that is {{cvt|10–12|cm}} in length and weighs around {{cvt|11.5|g}}. Male painted finches have a red forehead and face that stand out in contrast to the black breast of individuals. A bright red patch in the middle of the breast is present, while the sides of the breast, belly and flanks are black with white spots. The tail and upper body and upper wing are a reddish-brown colour. The rump and uppertail coverts are also mostly red and highly conspicuous when the birds is in flight. Painted finches have a long, slender and pointed bill which in the male the upper mandible is mostly black with a red tip and the lower mandible is mostly red with light blue-grey patches on either side of the base. The Iris of males is a cream or off-white colour and the legs of males vary between a dark brown to pinkish colour.<ref name="del hoyo">{{cite book|last1=del Hoyo|first1=J|last2=Elliott|first2=A|last3=Christie|first3=D. A|title=Handbook of the birds of the world.|date=2010|publisher=Lynx Edicions|location=Barcelona|isbn=978-84-96553-68-2}}</ref><ref name="forshaw">{{cite book|last1=Forshaw|first1=J|last2=Shephard|first2=M|last3=Pridham|first3=A|title=Grassfinches in Australia|date=2012|publisher=CSIRO|location=Collingwood, Australia|isbn=978-0-643-09634-9}}</ref>

Females look similar to males, although the red colouration on the face is duller and is restricted to the lores, cheeks and around the eyes. The red patch present on the breast is also duller when compared to the males as are the underparts that are generally a duller brownish-black. The underparts on females though are more spotted. The bill of females is quite similar to males but usually with less red on upper mandible. The iris is also a cream or off-white colour and legs once again vary between dark brown to a pinkish colour.<ref name="del hoyo" /><ref name="forshaw" />

Juvenile painted finches are similar in appearance to females but their underparts are duller and browner. Juveniles lack the red colouration on their face and their bill is black which becomes paler and almost pinkish on the lower part. Juvenile's eyes are a grey-brown.<ref name="del hoyo" /><ref name="forshaw" />

==Ecology==

===Behaviour===
Painted finches usually occur in pairs and small flocks; however, larger flocks of up to 100 individuals have been seen when congregating around water sources. These large flocks may contain other species of finches and honeyeaters.<ref name="del hoyo" /><ref name="forshaw" /><ref name="carter">{{cite journal|last1=Carter|first1=T|title=Exploration of the North West cape|journal=Emu|date=1902|volume=2|issue=2|pages=77–84|doi=10.1080/01584197.1902.11791887|bibcode=1902EmuAO...2...77C|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/part/378922}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Mules|first1=M. W.|title=Notes on some birds in the vicinity of Coniston Station, Central Australia|journal=South Australian Ornithologist|date=1932|volume=11|pages=212–221|url=http://www.birdssa.asn.au/images/saopdfs/Volume11/1932V11P212.pdf}}</ref> Painted finches are generally less vocal than other Australian grassfinches; however, its calls are among the loudest and harshest of this species group.<ref name="Immelman3" /> Its contact calls have been described as sounding like ''trut, chek-chek'' or ''ced up, cheddy-up''. When alone, males often make a loud wheezing and chattering song, females make a rattling call in response to danger near the nest.<ref name="Immelman3" /><ref name="del hoyo" /> 
<ref name="forshaw" />

===Diet===
The painted finch feeds on grass seeds mostly from spinifex ([[Triodia (plant)|Triodia]]) species and sometimes may consume fruit and blades of grass. Individuals forage on the ground, hopping and bouncing among rocks and tussocks of grass in order to glean for fallen seeds.<ref name="cayley" /><ref name="Immelman3" /> Captive birds have seen to sally-strike flying termites and glean aphids from vegetation; however, feeding on insects in the wild has not been observed.<ref name="Immelman3" />

===Reproduction===
If conditions are suitable painted finches breed at almost any time of year with eggs being recorded in all months except November and December, and nestlings being recorded between March and October.<ref name="cayley" /><ref name="Immelman3" /><ref name="johnstone">{{cite journal|last1=Johnstone|first1=R.E.|last2=Burbidge|first2=Allan H.|last3=Stone|first3=P.|title=Birds of the southern Carnarvon Basin, Western Australia: distribution, status and historical changes|journal=Records of the Western Australian Museum, Supplement|date=2000|volume=60|issue=1|page=371|doi=10.18195/issn.0313-122x.61.2000.371-448|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name="kovac">{{cite journal|last1=Kovac|first1=N. J.|last2=Niejalke|first2=D. P.|title=Observation and breeding records of the painted finch Emblema pictum associated with artesian springs in South Australia|journal=South Australian Ornithologist|date=2004|volume=34|pages=181–182}}</ref> Courtship usually occurs on the ground and involves both sexes picking up twigs or other items and dropping them. A greeting display may replace this which involves the male singing to the female in a vertical posture, raising its body feathers and pivoting its head from side to side.<ref name="Immelman3" /><ref name="del hoyo" /><ref name="forshaw" />

Painted finches are monogamous and nest in simple pairs.<ref name="Hornsby">{{cite journal|last1=Hornsby|first1=P|title=Nest building by the painted firetail in the north Flinders Ranges of South Australia|journal=South Australian Ornithologist|date=1987|volume=30|page=83|url=http://www.birdssa.asn.au/images/saopdfs/Volume30/1987V30P083A.pdf}}</ref> Nesting sites are usually well concealed clumps of spinifex grass, however pairs have been seen to nest in natural cavities near the top of a clump or on the ground at the base. Nesting sites may occasionally also include other species of tussock grasses or in low shrubs.<ref name="cayley" /><ref name="kovac" /><ref name="Hornsby" /> Individuals build loose, bottle shaped or domed nests with a wide entrance and usually consists of stems of spinifex but may also contain other grass stems, twigs or rootlets. The nest is often lined with feathers, plant down, hairy seeds, grass, wool, fur or balls of fluff.<ref name="del hoyo" /><ref name="kovac" /><ref name="Hornsby" /> The opening of the nest is often decorated with a piece of charcoal.<ref name="forshaw" />

Females will lay a clutch size of 3 to 5 oval white eggs. Eggs sometimes have a bluey tinge and are incubated for around 13–14 days by both the male and female.<ref name="cayley" /><ref name="Immelman3" /><ref name="del hoyo" /><ref name="forshaw" /> Young are [[altricial]] and do not leave the nest until they are around 21–26 days old.<ref name="del hoyo" /><ref name="forshaw" /><ref>{{cite book|last1=Mobbs|first1=A. J.|title=The complete book of Australian finches|date=1990|publisher=T.F.H.|location=Neptune City, N.J.|isbn=978-0-7938-0295-1}}</ref> Young are fed for around two more weeks and they begin to develop courtship behaviour at 10 weeks old.<ref name="del hoyo" />

==Habitat and distribution==
The painted finch is found in arid and semi-arid zones, in rocky areas with a ground cover of spinifex grass.<ref name="del hoyo" /><ref name="forshaw" /><ref name="johnstone" /> The painted finch can be found in [[Western Australia]], [[Northern Australia]], [[Queensland]] and [[South Australia]]  <ref name="johnstone" /><ref name="kovac" /><ref name="holmes">{{cite journal|last1=Holmes|first1=G|last2=Noske|first2=R. A.|title=New locality records of birds in Arnhem Land and southern Gulf of Carpentaria|journal=Northern Territory Naturalist|date=1990|volume=12|pages=13–19|doi=10.5962/p.295586|doi-access=free}}</ref> In 2007 and 2008 large influxes of painted finches entered into western [[New South Wales]]. These individuals have since been recorded breeding and a resident population is now thought to have been established.<ref name="McAllan">{{cite journal|last1=McAllan|first1=I. A. W.|last2=Knight|first2=B. J.|last3=O'Brien|first3=R. M.|last4=Parker|first4=D. G.|last5=Ingwersen|first5=D. A.|title=The painted finch in New South Wales.|journal=Australian Field Ornithology|date=2010|volume=27|issue=1|pages=10–18|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/274638963}}</ref>

== Conservation status ==
On the [[IUCN Red List|IUCN Red list]] the painted finch is listed as being of "Least Concern",<ref name="iucn"/> and this is also its listing in [[Queensland]] under the [[Nature Conservation Act 1992|Nature Conservation Act of 1992]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Species profile—''Emblema pictum'' (painted finch) |url=https://apps.des.qld.gov.au/species-search/details/?id=1374 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=Species information |publisher=Queensland Government}}</ref>

zh.extract:
彩火尾雀（学名：Emblema pictum），是梅花雀科的一种，为澳大利亚的特有种。全球活动范围约为1,300,000平方千米。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
彩火尾雀的平均体重约为11.2克。栖息地包括干燥的稀树草原、乡村花园、亚热带或热带的（低地）干燥疏灌丛和亚热带或热带的（低地）干草原。


参考文献

Emblema pictum. BirdLife.org.   [2011-06-27]. （原始内容存档于2016-03-04）.
